scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced a normal retail order only when item, price, promotion, shipment, and completion evidence aligned across the named systems of record.
Incomplete order and planogram prerequisites produced a blocked-state exception note instead of improvised execution.
The lane refused closure without proof-of-execution artifacts and preserved an auditable open state.
Conflicting ERP, WMS, ASN, and retailer receiving evidence stayed unresolved and was escalated rather than masked as successful execution.
Quality hold and release boundaries remained intact even under promotional urgency.
Supplier or item substitution approval was rejected and routed to the correct adjacent owners.
Short-ship and promo-pricing defects were moved into rework handling with explicit evidence and next-owner routing.
Commercial price and concession authority was treated as out of scope and handed off cleanly.
The lane preserved realistic CPG retail vocabulary and artifact shapes instead of falling back to generic operations language.
Missing tenant authority and policy context triggered low-confidence escalation rather than confident synthesis.
Conflicting public guidance and tenant or retailer policy were recorded as a precedence conflict that required escalation.
Tenant-specific franchise, approved-item, and hold controls were applied without breaking the public baseline or inventing new lane authority.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold with complete ability coverage for the execution-processing-and-fulfillment and manufacturing-and-supply-chain contract.
The package stays inside CPG retail execution and does not claim final quality release, supplier approval, or commercial concession authority.
